author: Theophilus of Antioch
interpretation: Theophilus reads Adam's sin as the failure of an immature being who was neither fully mortal nor immortal but poised between the two. Had Adam obeyed, he would have progressed to immortality. The prohibition was not arbitrary punishment but a developmental test suited to Adam's childlike state. Death entered not as divine vengeance but as the natural consequence of turning from the source of life.
context: Theophilus's 'immature Adam' motif influenced Irenaeus and the Eastern theological tradition, which generally sees the Fall as a failure to mature rather than the corruption of an originally perfect being.

author: Augustine of Hippo
interpretation: Augustine develops the doctrine of original sin from Genesis 3. Adam and Eve's disobedience was not merely a mistake but a fundamental act of pride — the will turning from God toward the self. This corrupted human nature at its root, transmitting guilt and a disordered will to all descendants through biological generation. Concupiscence — the inability to not sin — is the inheritance of every human born after Adam.
context: Augustine formulated original sin primarily in debate with Pelagius, who taught that humans could achieve righteousness by natural effort. Augustine's reading of Genesis 3 became the foundation of Western Christian anthropology and profoundly shaped both Catholic and Protestant theology.

author: John Chrysostom
interpretation: Chrysostom reads the Fall as a failure of moral vigilance. The serpent targeted Eve not because she was weaker by nature but because she was unsuspecting and unguarded. Adam's greater fault was passive compliance — he was present and silent. Chrysostom emphasizes the progressive nature of temptation: the serpent begins with a question, escalates to contradiction of God, then offers an alternative vision.
context: Chrysostom's moral-practical reading contrasts with Augustine's metaphysical-doctrinal approach. The Eastern tradition generally followed Chrysostom in focusing on personal moral responsibility rather than inherited guilt.

author: Anselm of Canterbury
interpretation: Anselm reads the Fall as creating an infinite debt of honor owed to God. Since sin against an infinite being carries infinite weight, no finite creature can repay it. Only a being who is both fully God and fully human can offer satisfaction sufficient to restore the broken relationship. Genesis 3 thus establishes the absolute necessity of the incarnation.
context: Anselm's satisfaction theory of atonement became the dominant Western model and directly shaped later Protestant substitutionary atonement theology. His reading makes Genesis 3 the problem to which the entire gospel is the answer.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin identifies the root sin as unfaithfulness — Adam and Eve defected from God's word by entertaining the serpent's counter-narrative. The specific act of eating was secondary to the underlying rebellion of elevating human judgment above divine command. Calvin traces total depravity to this moment: every human faculty — reason, will, affections — was corrupted, leaving no spiritual capacity untouched.
context: Calvin's emphasis on total depravity as rooted in Genesis 3 became a cornerstone of Reformed theology. His focus on unbelief as the root sin (rather than pride or sensuality) shifted the locus from act to disposition.

author: Martin Luther
interpretation: Luther reads the serpent's strategy as a theological method still in use: first cast doubt on God's word ('Did God really say?'), then deny its consequences ('You will not surely die'), then offer an alternative promise ('You will be like God'). He sees this as the pattern of every false teaching. The Fall reveals that human reason, once it abandons the word of God, inevitably invents idolatrous alternatives.
context: Luther's reading is deeply pastoral and polemical — he saw the serpent's method replicated in papal claims to interpretive authority that contradicted the plain sense of Scripture.

author: Gerhard von Rad
interpretation: Von Rad reads the Fall narrative as Israel's mature theological reflection on the universal human condition. The story is not primarily about a historical first couple but about the perpetual human situation: the desire for autonomy from God, the lure of forbidden knowledge, and the inevitable consequences of broken trust. The Yahwist author crafted a narrative of extraordinary psychological insight.
context: Von Rad's existential reading influenced a generation of Old Testament scholars who read Genesis 3 as anthropological reflection rather than historical reportage, though he maintained its theological seriousness.

author: G.K. Beale
interpretation: Beale reads the Fall as a failure of Adam's priestly vocation. As the guardian of the garden-temple, Adam should have expelled the serpent — an unclean intruder into sacred space. Instead he abdicated his role, allowed the desecration of holy ground, and was himself expelled. The rest of Scripture traces the long restoration of faithful priesthood, culminating in Christ who successfully resists temptation and guards the new creation.
context: Beale's temple-guardian reading connects Genesis 3 to the broader biblical pattern of faithful versus unfaithful priesthood, providing a typological framework that runs through Leviticus, the monarchy, and into the New Testament.
